The figure below shows one use case: the system creates for a procured component supplier parts. For these three suppliers you have instructed them to use exact basic material for steel XYZ. The suppliers won't give any compliance information to you. You plug-in the basic material to the component. Now the system considers both the basic material and the supplier parts and states "missing data". With the "prefer basic material" normalization, the supplier parts get suppressed in case there exists a basic material.

Product Compliance Structure - normalizations

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Summary

SAP Product and REACH Compliance is a powerful tool. For my customers I have created process documentations with 200+ pages, for sure this cannot be added into a blog. However, the basic concept on a high level is given.

In case of questions or required help just contact the author.

entry.title: Introducing New Safety Data Sheet Features
entry.content:
Here are the highlights for the newest features for Safety Data Sheet Management:

  • SDS Generation extended to include sections 9, 11 and 12
  • New Business Event between Partners and SAP BTP: Request Data Calculation
  • New API’s to move data from legacy systems into SAP S/4HANA for product compliance
    • GHS Classification
    • Handling and Storage
    • Safety Related Properties
  • Versioning of GHS assessments for substances
  • Compliance Requirement topic to group requirements together when based on the same compliance pattern
  • Waste Code compliance requirements versions now have aspect definitions available
  • New Aerosol Labeling app

 

In this post we will go through the system and see how to maintain and review basic data for our example industrial cleaner.

Industrial Cleaner Example

Here is the Work View, where we have an overview of the processing status of the different data under each section.

 

From the Work View we can navigate to the different sections for specific information including History and Admin Data.

Section 9 - Safety-Related Properties

We can also check our information from Section 3 - Composition / Information on Ingredients with the analytical composition.

Then we can get down to the substance level via the Manage Substances Compliance app.

 

Here we are interested in the properties and can view the Toxicological Information which has already been released.

The new Business Event on requesting data calculations enables partners to consume business events running data calculations with public API’s to read and write the dependent datasets.

 

Along with the new Compliance Requirement for the EU Waste Code, you can see here the new user interface for setting the detailed information.

For Aerosol Labeling as new app variant, under the Manage Regulatory Data app, has been developed to manage data for unpackaged products and raw materials. This accompanies a new compliance requirement for Aerosol Labeling based on the Regulatory Data Assessment compliance pattern. For more information check this out.

entry.title: Exporting and Importing Custom Objects and CPE Packages in SAP Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
This blog provides a broad outline of how custom objects like custom dimensions, custom measures and custom metrics can be exported and imported between different tenants, as well as the exporting and importing of CPE packages.

Restriction: Currently, we have no option to export and import the following objects.

  • KPI Tiles
  • Ambitions
  • Ambitions Groups
  • Leading and supported reporting hierarchy

Fact Sheet: Once we have defined our own set of custom dimensions, custom measures and custom metrics, we can export that set using either of the respective apps, i.e. Manage Custom Dimensions, Manage Measures, Manage Metrics. We can also export and import the created CPE (Calculation and Processing Engine) content.

To begin with, let us focus on how we can export and import custom objects. For Instance I have created a custom dimension called "Insurance Type For Employees" and updated the master data. 

Exporting and Importing Custom Objects:

Now let us choose "Go To Export" option.

Add the desired custom objects.

Select the desired custom dimension from the value help and add to the custom objects inventory.

All valid custom objects can be exported using the option "Export All".

we can find the exported file in the "Downloads" folder with the file name as "sct-custom-inventory.json" and can be renamed if required for import.

Now, let us access "Manage Custom Dimensions" app to import.

choose the file to upload,

Let us choose the option "Import All". System imports the valid custom objects.

 

Exporting and Importing CPE Packages:

Let us look at how we can export and import content from CPE packages. We can enter CPE from various possible ways. For instance I have chosen using app "CPE Process Templates for Data Management".

Let us move on to environments overview page,

from the overview page, we can select the desired package to export by choosing the option "Export".

For instance, I have chosen "SCT - Manage ESG Data", " Version 0009".

A file in the .zip file format is added to the list with the name and version of the environment. We can view the exported environment.

 

 

Now, let us import using file upload option.

After the file upload has been successful, we must choose the option "Import" and input the details.

Let us choose "Import" and check the import status.

 

 😊This brings me to the end of the "Exporting and Importing Custom Objects" walkthrough process in SAP Sustainability Control Tower. I hope this blog proves useful to the Sustainability Control Tower Team. I think this feature brings a significant value to the business during implementation process.

If you want to gain a deep understanding of  SAP Sustainability Control Tower and out of the box seamless integration with SAP EHSM, Environment Management, check out my SAP SCT & EHSM blogs mentioned below, they will give you a broad outline of SAP Sustainability Control Tower  & SAP EHSM Environment Management topics.

  1. European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) 
  2. EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-1 
  3. EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-2 
  4. EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-3 
  5. SAP Sustainability Control Tower-Import Greenhouse Gas Emissions from SAP EHS Environment Management 
  6. Addendum to SAP Sustainability Control Tower-Import Greenhouse Gas Emissions from SAP EHSM-EM 
  7. SAP Sustainability Control Tower(SCT) Metric Management-Basic Custom Metric & Standard Custom Metric 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy.C

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: ESRS S1-6 – Characteristics of the undertaking’s employees
entry.content:
Disclaimer: This blog post reflects the state of the SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) as of February 11th, 2025.

ESRS S1-6 requires a company to disclose quantitative and qualitative (/narrative) information about its employees. SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) provides out of the box several quantitative metrics, hence this article focusses on how to bridge the gap for the ESRS S1-6 requirements for the quantitative data points. Specifically, the focus is on paragraph 50 a: "the undertaking shall disclose the total number of employees by head count, and breakdowns by gender and by country for countries in which the undertaking has 50 or more employees representing at least 10% of its total number of employees." 

Total Number of Employees

For the “total number of employees by head count” the SAP provided metric Total Number of Employees can be used.  

Breakdown by Gender

The breakdown by gender is currently not out of the box possible in SCT. In the additional application requirement 55 (AR 55) it is described that the company shall disclose the information in a tabular format and provides the following template:

As of now SCT doesn’t allow to display tables. However, it is possible to visualize the breakdown by gender with Standard Custom Metrics. You can create a Standard Custom Metric within the Manage Metrics app:

Then you can define the details of the Standard Custom Metric. In this case, for Z_1-6_FEMALE:

Please ensure that your leading and supported reporting hierarchies are set correctly.

I.e., if you provide information via the Measure “Workforce Headcount” and its template “Workforce Headcount – Actuals” in the Manage ESG Data app, you need to ensure that the respective hierarchies are maintained if you want them to be supported in SCT.

  • ID_COMPANY_CODE stands for Legal Entity
  • ID_ORG_UNIT stands for Reporting Line
  • ID_BUSINESS_LOC stands for Business Location

In the example above all hierarchies are provided with data and hence should be supported.

In the calculation definition you can select the Measure “Workforce Headcount”, the Calculation Method is Sum, and you select the respective filter to sum up. In this case you sum up all entries with Gender Female.  As the requirement is to disclose the headcount it is crucial to set the Value Type to Row Count.

Background: In your template you define both, MEASURE_VALUE_CHAR and MEASURE_COUNT. For the “Workforce Headcount” the former defines the full time equivalent (FTE) of each entry whereas the latter defines the headcount. In this simplified example there are in total 275 FTEs and the headcount is 350. This can be the case due to part time working employees. When defining the calculation in SCT the value type defines whether is should sum up the aggregated values (in this case the MEASURE_VALUE_CHAR – FTE) or the rows (in this case MEASURE_COUNT – headcount).

After creating the Standard Custom Metric and uploading the “Workforce Headcount – Actuals” template in the Manage ESG Data app, you can then calculate the data for the Standard Custom Metrics, and publish it. E.g., for Z_S1-6_FEMALE:

After the calculation you can already see that SCT selected the correct entries to be summed up:

In the Analyze ESG Data app you can then visualize all calculated Standard Metrics and SAP provided Metrics:

Breakdown by country for countries in which the undertaking has 50 or more employees representing at least 10% of its total number of employees.

The breakdown by country with the respective filtering of 50+ employees and representing at least 10% of the company’s total number of employees is currently not out of the box possible in SCT. In the additional application requirement 55 (AR 55) it is described that the company shall disclose the information in a tabular format and provides the following template:

As of now SCT doesn’t allow to display tables. However, it is possible to visualize the breakdown by countries with the Our Ambitions app. If you drill down the Total Number of Employees Metric under the Business Location hierarchy, you can visualize the breakdown per country. Please note that this requires the appropriate set-up of the master data and the provided actuals. Furthermore, a filtering of 50+ employees and representing at least 10% of the company’s total number of employees is also here not possible.

entry.title: The ABAP Detective and the Errant Electrolyte
entry.content:
An aspect of sustainability is minimal resource use, in both the sense of lower costs, but also the avoidance of energy use that contributes to carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels. For an establishment on "all-electric" the latter is what might be called an externality. As a code and systems analysis detective, I look for areas of improvement.

Previous investigations into environmental monitoring tools are noted at the end; this case focuses on the efficiency and use of domestic hot water delivery. In the backwaters of the united states, standard appliances include a 30 or 40 gallon (sorry: X liters) tank with a heating source constantly attempting to maintain a set minimum temperature. Other areas use the "on-demand" approach, or hot-water-as-a-service, with a heating device close to the delivery point (let's not go into the 110 versus 220 volt infrastructure as a distraction).

To the crux of the biscuit: I set up a temperature monitor directly above the output of the hot water tank, connected to a Raspberry Pi that is polled by a central system. Other approaches would be locally collecting the data and uploading in scheduled bursts, pushing rather than pulling.

TMP102 gauge, copper, insulation. (Photo by Jim Spath)

(photo by Jim Spath)

This ad hoc wiring approach works in a non-intrusive way, as the sensor unit does not touch the copper pipes, nor contact running water. Typically throughout the day, hot water is not running, so the readings are close to ambient room temperature. When a tap is opened somewhere, preheated water goes near enough to tick up the temperature reading, then after the flows stops the temperature gradually degrades. Or, if the tank completely empties of heated water, the readings will correspondingly decline.

Standalone sensor:

TMP102 gauge with blips

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

A few times during the day, temperature rises, then declines. Not a particularly useful data set if you want to ask questions like "how hot is the hot water" or "how long did the washer run?" or even "how much did this unit of work cost?". The human brain can glance at these shapes and discern patterns, like Sherlock Holmes checking out cigar-ash-flakes in a pursuit. Writing an algorithm is a bit trickier.

The Zabbix open source suite I'm using has tools to run calculations on data feeds; for instance in some cases I'd pull values in Celsius, then add a derived parameter converting to Fahrenheit. There are ways to "look back" to prior values to determine deltas (which I've not implemented yet). You can also set up triggers to perform actions such as alerting on worst case scenarios (like overheating or frozen pipes: Ow!).

I'll gloss over the gnarly details of the Sparkfun/QWIIC monitoring hardware/software and cut to the chase: only one specific gauge per Pi, without adding multiplexing I2C complexities, so I was faced with deploying 2 Pi's with the same gauge type, or add a different gauge on 1 Pi. I chose the latter. Then the case went into what we colloquially called a "mung" state. Not "hung" but not right either.

Pi and the OS

If I could, I'd "flame" here, showing the rapid pace of software development breaks things that worked a few short years ago in surprising ways. I'm using code that someone else wrote in bash shell scripting back in 2012 or so, and that logic doesn't need to be tweaked when a new Linux kernel or even a subsystem component is written. And generally, Python examples the vendors share keeps working from year to year. Upgrades to the running environment are non-breaking other than minor steps like "oh, use Python 3.12 now instead of 3.10" that are easy enough to resolve.

My first Pi platform had a "6.1" 32-bit Linux kernel version; more recently new installs have a "6.6" kernel, where 64-bit flavors are as stable as the 32-bit. Great, why would that matter? Somewhere in the matrix, Python grew an appendage, nicknamed "venv" meaning a virtual environment. Let's add containers, crates, and virtualization while we're at it. Except this is a $10 machine with a single purpose.

TMP102 and BME280

These 2 temperature sensors are available in the chosen "ecosystem" that connects the Pi brain to the digital sensors, which could also include humidity and barometric pressure. Since I had collected and deployed at least one of each, I assumed connecting both to 1 Pi would be simple. I had code samples in shell and Python for both. "Just" follow the manufacturers instructions and "pip" it into place (no, not the old DEC PIP peripheral to peripheral connector, the new python software package installer). Failure to launch:

 

$ pip install qwiic_bme280
error: externally-managed-environment

 

Oh, darn, this didn't work, and spit out a bunch of scary messages, especially this last dooms-day-scenario:

 

You can override this, at the risk of breaking \
 your Python installation or OS,  \
 by passing --break-system-packages. \
hint: See PEP 668 for the detailed specification.

 

"Break system packages" sounds ominous. Maybe don't take that approach. 

pip breakage

(screenshot from terminal session)

A more staid detective would go read the reference manuals, consult others online, re-read the source code, then diligently make necessary repairs. Others more reactive might literally check with their vendors and ask what's up with this. Investigators with a hacking tendency would start typing likely fixes and rout/route around the damage. Command line use of a venv turned out to be fairly simple:

 

python3 -m venv venv
venv/bin/pip3 install sparkfun-qwiic-bme280

[...]
Successfully installed \
 smbus2-0.5.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-bme280-0.9.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-i2c-1.0.0

 

No doubt more experienced Python developers are aghast at the hammer approach I used.

Getting readings at the command line would now work with 2 sensors; the new one shows:

 

$ venv/bin/python3.11  /path/to/sparkfun.py  temperature_celsius
16.2118

 

Fine, a 3-pipe problem rather than a trivial 2-pipe one. Except the whole intent is to automate sensor data collection, not have to run a command line manually. I'll leave out the messy trial-and-error details, other than to share that the Zabbix process debug steps boil down to seeing whether a parameter shows as "not supported" or returns an error message such as "got string, expecting a number" in the console, and checking the agent logs for clues (like: "The Qwiic BME280 device isn't connected to the system. Please check your connection" when I used the wrong sensor).

Stuffed inside an old shell script are new links to the monitor logic.

 

/path/venv/python3 /path/sparkfun-venv.py temperature_celsius

 

The middle section didn't need to have any virtual environment goo but for the sake of my future self looking at these sensors after the next incompatibility will possibly recall why.

Before going to the trouble of writing calculation or trigger steps in parameter definitions I used a handy UI feature in Zabbix that overlays 2 measurements in one chart. First, make sure both metrics are stable:

2 charts

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Then, add the second parameter to a dashboard widget. I didn't muck around with colors, lines, or transparencies, taking the defaults as good enough for the quick quality control / QC check.

2 temperature sensors on 1 chart

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Next problems to solve?

  • The 2 gauges differ under "no flow" states by over 1 degree.
  • Alerts/triggers
  • Validation/calibration

Calibration

Back in the last century, my polytechnic/polymath education included calibrating thermometers by determining how close their readings were to freezing boiling water temperatures. A memorable lesson in theory versus practice, tossing aside the novice assumption that a device always shows accurate values. How many degrees or tenths-of-degrees plus or minus the correct reading? (let's skip over side issues such as does water boil at exactly 100 degrees Celsius at any specific elevation or location).

What I'd probably investigate next is recording the temperature at the taps compared to the utility room infrastructure, particularly after hearing anecdotal comments such as "the kitchen sink faucet is not hot enough."

NOTES / REFERENCES

Shell:

  • https://znil.net/index.php/Zabbix:Template_Raspberry_Pi
  • https://znil.net/index.php?title=Temperatur_/_Spannung_etc._des_Raspberry_Pi_selbst_auslesen

Python:

  • http://rptl.io/venv
  • https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
  • https://peps.python.org/pep-0668/
  • https://github.com/sparkfun/qwiic_bme280_py

SAP Community posts:

  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Summer Heat
  • The ABAP Detective Gets Their Clock Cleaned
  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Heat, or How To Cleanse Data

Also:

  • Intra home detectors

entry.title: Unlocking Scope 3 Transparency with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange - Deep Dive & Demo
entry.content:
SAP Sustainability Data Exchange (SDX) provides a seamless, automated solution for exchanging product carbon footprint (PCF) data, ensuring compliance with global standards and enabling data-driven sustainability decisions.

If you’re a sustainability lead, supply chain professional, or technical expert working on emissions tracking, this deep dive session is essential viewing. It offers a comprehensive walkthrough of SDX’s capabilities and how businesses can implement it to scale sustainability reporting.

Key Benefits of SAP Sustainability Data Exchange

SAP SDX is designed to facilitate efficient, accurate, and standardized PCF data sharing across the value chain. Here's how:

1. Standardized, Interoperable Data Exchange

  • Built to support industry standards like WBCSD PACT and Catena-X, ensuring data compatibility across networks.
  • Maintains data sovereignty, allowing companies to control their shared data.

2. Efficient Supplier Data Requests

  • Buyers can request supplier-specific PCF data for purchased products.
  • Bulk request capabilities streamline data collection from multiple partners at once.

3. Centralized PCF Inventory & Audit Readiness

  • Consolidated view of all purchased and finished product footprints.
  • Ensures compliance with ESG regulations and simplifies audit processes.

For a more detailed technical breakdown, explore the official Deep Dive PDF.

Why You Should Watch the Deep Dive Session

Understanding the full potential of SAP SDX is crucial for businesses looking to:


✔ Move from estimated to actual emissions data and improve reporting accuracy.
✔ Ensure compliance with evolving ESG regulations.
✔ Identify CO₂ reduction hotspots and make informed decisions.
✔ Automate emissions tracking across complex supply chains.

This on-demand deep dive video will walk you through real use cases, live demos, and best practices for implementing SDX in your sustainability strategy. Watch it here.

Take the Next Step – Try SAP SDX for Free

To experience SDX firsthand, SAP offers a free 30-day trial where you can explore its functionalities with sample data and guided processes. This is your opportunity to see how SDX can help your business enhance supply chain transparency and streamline carbon reporting.

➡ Start your free trial now!

Have questions or looking for implementation guidance? Reach out to us—we’re here to help you drive sustainability forward. 🚀

Curious and want to learn more?

Visit SAP.com/SDX  for more exciting product information. We are now supporting digital sales experience! You can also find out more detailed guides on Help Portal and the future capabilities on the Roadmap Explorer.

Partners can also access the latest assets on the Partner Portal.

See how our reference customer WITTE Automotive talk about their decarbonization journey with SAP Sustainability solutions. 

**************************

Jessica Bartels, Product Marketing Lead - SAP Sustainability Data Exchange

#sapsustainabilitydataexchange #sapfootprintmanagement #sapsustainabilitycontroltower #sapbusinessnetwork

entry.title: Teaching Business Process Simulation and Sustainability in Procurement
entry.content:
Companies need to continuously evaluate and optimize their business processes and ensure critical supply chain operations. As a result, Business Process Management (BPM) has become integral to both academic curricula and industry practice. Yet, while BPM techniques like process modeling and mining are well-established in education, business process simulation is often overlooked. This blog post explores a new approach to teaching process simulation within the context of sustainability in procurement, leveraging SAP Signavio to enrich student learning.

SAP Signavio Process Simulation Curriculum

Business process simulation—analyzing “what-if” scenarios to optimize operations—helps students and professionals gain insights into process efficiency, bottlenecks, and optimization strategies, making it a critical yet underutilized tool in BPM education.

To foster data-driven decision making and process simulation skills, we developed a process simulation case study that focuses on sustainability in procurement.* Using SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite, academic edition, our case study extends an existing ERP simulation teaching case by adding an extra subprocess for supplier selection:

Sustainable Procurement Case Study

In our teaching case, A Company that Makes Apparel (ACM Apparel) is navigating new European Union regulations on sustainability due diligence in supply chains. The case walks learners through the traditional procure-to-pay (P2P) process and then challenges them to optimize this process with sustainability and efficiency considerations using IT systems. Students engage with real-world scenarios and develop process optimization skills crucial in today’s fast-paced business environment.

Hands-On Learning with SAP Signavio

The simulation case is designed to be interactive and adaptable, allowing educators to adjust the complexity based on course needs. Students begin by modeling a manual supplier selection process, learning about the S2P and P2P processes using a real-world scenario. They then simulate their supplier selection process using SAP Signavio, comparing the outcomes of different scenarios to identify inefficiencies.

While the sustainability due diligence increases the time and cost of supplier selection, students are guided to re-engineer this the process, automating and integrating process steps using SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network. This reduces costs and ensures compliance with sustainability standards.

Get started with SAP Signavio Simulation

If you’re and educator wanting to explore SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite for education, you can register for an unlimited free academic workspace using your university e-mail address at academic.signavio.com.

If you want to start teaching sustainability and process re-enigneering using simulation in SAP Signavio, you can download the teaching slide deck. In case of questions, feel free to reach out to signavio.academics@sap.com!

You can find the complete case description in the paper "Teaching Business Process Simulation and Sustainability in Procurement" that was published in the conference proceedings of the 2024 SAP Academic Community Conference D-A-CH.

*The curriculum has been validated by industry practitioners and tested in educational settings, showing significant potential in enhancing BPM education.

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: Calculating a Corporate Carbon Footprint
entry.content:
Disclaimer: This blog post reflects the state of the SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) as of March 10th, 2025.

In today's business world, sustainability has become a critical focus, not just due to regulatory demands but also from genuine corporate accountability and environmental stewardship. With both intrinsic motivations to reduce carbon emissions and regulatory requirements such as the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS E1-6) mandating the disclosure of Scope 1, 2, and 3 total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, companies face an urgent call to action. SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) offers a comprehensive solution to support businesses in this dual pursuit, providing a robust tool for calculating and managing their corporate carbon footprint (CCF). By leveraging SCT, organizations can align their sustainability strategies with both their own environmental goals and compliance needs. This article explores a simple example on how SCT empowers businesses to seamlessly integrate sustainability into their core operations, meeting regulatory standards while advancing their intrinsic commitment to a sustainable future.

Before you start with the CCF calculation you need to have completed the technical setup and have the appropriate user roles. For details, see:

  • Enabling Corporate Carbon Footprint Calculation
  • User Roles

This CCF process can be divided into 4 subtasks:

1. Set-up relevant Master Data

Manage Master Data: SCT allows you to set-up your company hierarchy by means of the Legal Entity (ID_COMPANY_CODE), Reporting Line (ID_ORG_UNIT) and Business Location (ID_BUSINESS_LOC). It is important to note that CCF is based on Business Locations. Therefore, you need to ensure that Business Locations are maintained. In this example we will focus on two Business Locations, Munich and Berlin:

Besides Business Locations it is important to note that SAP relies on the Emissions DPI to store the GHG information. All relevant dimensions such as Allocation Method, Scope, Scope Category, Scope Subcategory are already maintained by SAP.

Manage Emission Factors: In the Manage Emissions Factors app you can see that SCT comes pre-delivered with Emission Factor (EF) data packages by the US government Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Beyond that it is possible to provide own EF data package. Therefore, an LCA Template or a Simple Template can be filled out. For calculating the CCF, the Simple Template is recommended.

Mandatory fields in the template are highlighted with *. In this simplified example you can find for instance the data package entry 1003 “Train Travel” with the UNIT_OF_MEASURE in KM, where 100KM in DE (Germany) result in 0.56 kgCO2e.

 

Once you have set-up the data package, you can release it.

2. Periodic Configuration

Manage Business Activities: A Business Activity can be any activity in your company that you want to calculate emissions for. One activity is Business Location specific. If you want to calculate your Scope 3.6 Business Travel emissions across your company, you will need to create the respective Business Activities for each Business Location.

In our simplified example we want to calculate the emissions from the following Business Activities from Berlin and Munich. You can therefore create the Business Activities directly in SCT or download a template and upload it, which will make sense for bigger organizations.

Let’s add another Business Activity for Train Travel for Munich. For the name of the Business Activity, it makes sense to include the location ID. You can select your GHG Category, Description, Location, Validity Period and the Emission Factor Periods.

Once you have updated the General Information part, you can maintain the Emission Factor Period.  

As we have provided the Emission Factor for Train Travel in our CUSTOMER_SPECIFIC data package, we can select it and relevant fields such as the Quantity, Quantity UoM and Total CO2e are prefilled automatically from the data package.

You can then select the Preferred Footprint Type from Quantity-Based, Spend-Based or Manual Emission Footprint. In this case we only maintain the Quantity-Based Footprint. If you have provided also the Spend-Based Footprint in your Emission Factor data package, you can maintain both Footprint Details the Business Activity.

Manage GHG Data Collections: Before you can finally start with the data collection and calculation itself you need to set-up the GHG Data Collection(s). With that you define the period and the Business Activities you want to collect data for every Business Location. In this case, The Footprint Inventory Scope has been created for Periods January, February and March whereas the first two months of 2024 have already been completed with the data collection.

When clicking into the newly created Footprint Inventory Scope, SCT sets up the GHG Data Forms for every Business Location. In our case Los Angeles, Mexico City and New York are not relevant, so we can easily scope them out by setting them to not applicable.

Furthermore, you can notice that after clicking on the Footprint Inventory Scope you have actually changed the App and are now in the GHG Data Collection. You can now click on any Business Location where you want to start your data collection for.

3. Collection and Calculation

Record GHG Data: If you want to collect your GHG Data, you have two options to get there. 1) as described above via the Manage GHG Data Collections App and then clicking on the respective Footprint Inventory Scope, in this case March 2024 or 2) via Record GHG Data Data App:

When clicking into one Business Location, you can then edit the entry and collect the data.

After entering the data, SCT might give a notification. In this case it notifies the user that the entered quantity differs by more than 30% compared to the previous period. In this case March 2024 is compared to February 2024 for the Business Location Berlin.

When you have provided both options to maintain the data Quantity-Based and Spend-Based, you can decide how you want to collect your data. In this case for Scope 3.6 long haul travel you can decide whether you want to enter the data via the flown kilometers, or the Euros spent. Once you have maintained one option, you cannot maintain the other one anymore.

  At the end you can save your data collection. After collecting all the relevant data and saving it you can see that the progress is now at 100% and you can set the data collection to complete.

Finally, in the Manage GHG Data Collections App you can review the data a final time and confirm the review. Once confirmed, the data collection is completed and reviewed and can then be visualized in SCT.

4. Reporting and Analytics

You can see that the data collection has been successfully completed in the following Apps: Manage ESG Data, Analyze ESG Data, Our Ambitions and in View Emission Dashboard:

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: Q1 2025 Updates & Highlights
entry.content:
For Chief Sustainability Officers, the recent Omnibus updates to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) ease immediate regulatory pressure and give organizations more time to adapt. Yet the core imperative remains unchanged: delivering measurable progress on sustainability goals, building resilience, and maintaining stakeholder trust.   

In today’s rapidly evolving regulatory landscape, organizations face mounting pressure to meet ambitious sustainability goals while managing cost, risk, and complexity across global operations. For CSOs, the mandate is clear: deliver measurable progress on sustainability goals, ensure compliance with evolving regulations, and embed sustainability across the enterprise. But fragmented data, limited visibility, and mounting disclosure demands make this easier said than done. SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) is purpose-built to support CSOs in leading with clarity and confidence—offering integrated intelligence across financial and non-financial performance, enabling comprehensive decision-making in real time, and driving an accelerated transition toward low-carbon, circular, and responsible business models.  

In this blog post, we’ll explore the latest Q1 2025 innovations in SCT—from new qualitative metrics and AI-driven report customization to expanded analytics and enhanced connectivity—designed to help sustainability leaders turn complexity into control and ambition into action. 

Qualitative Metrics Definition and Data Collection App 

You can create qualitative metrics and collect textual information for a selected time period according to the configured period granularity. The system supports defining narrative, semi-narrative, and Boolean reference types and mapping data points according to the disclosure requirements of the reporting standard version at the metric level. 

Afterward, you can use the new “Manage ESG Qualitative Data” App to collect data for qualitative metrics. By selecting the metric ID and report period, disclosure information relevant to a data point can be added for the period selected. 

 

Enhanced Metric Configuration  

New fields are added to the Metric Configuration to allow a more detailed configuration and reporting: "Reporting Standard Version", "Disclosure Requirement"," Disclosure References". Multiple selection of disclosure references is possible and the content for mapping to cover ESRS and EU Taxonomy is included. You can extend this content to add other standards. 

In Q1 2025, SCT introduces the ability to define custom metrics with up to five dimensions for deeper drill-down and analysis, improving extensibility and reducing the need to recreate metrics for each new dimension. This enhancement is supported by a new public Read OData API, enabling secure access to standard custom metrics data—including additional dimension breakdowns—without requiring recalculation outside of SCT. Customers can configure and maintain these metrics in the Manage Metrics app, which includes classification fields (such as Reporting Standard Version and Disclosure Reference) for better alignment with frameworks like European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) and EU Taxonomy. Once configured, these custom metrics can be consumed in the new Analyze ESG Data app, other SAC integrations, or third-party tools using the updated APIs. By streamlining metric definitions and exposing key metadata (for example, metric formulas, units of measure, and leading hierarchies), SCT empowers users to leverage more accurate and granular sustainability insights that support regulatory compliance and organizational reporting needs.  

 

Expanded SAC Live Connectivity Enhances ESG Reporting 

In November 2024, SCT expanded its SAC Live Connectivity feature to support all 12 Data Provider Interfaces (DPIs), significantly enhancing the integration between SAP Sustainability Control Tower and SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). Initially launched in Q2 2024 with support for GHG Emissions, this expansion now includes comprehensive connectivity for Emissions, Energy, Resource Use, Water, Waste, Land, People, Injury, Corruption Incidents, Prosperity, and General Measures. This development allows customers with SAC licenses to seamlessly access and visualize their sustainability data in real-time without the need for data replication. By exposing custom fields and enabling live connectivity across all DPIs, users can create dynamic and detailed dashboards tailored to their specific ESG reporting needs. Additionally, a new outbound API, SustainabilityCustomMetricsData, is now available in the Custom Metrics category on SAP Business Accelerator Hub, allowing users to retrieve data for standard custom metrics, including drill-down dimensions. This enhancement not only improves the flexibility and reach of sustainability reporting in alignment with ESRS but also streamlines the data integration process, empowering organizations to make more informed and timely decisions based on accurate and up-to-date ESG metrics.  

 

Instance-based Authorizations 

We’re excited to introduce instance-based authorizations in SAP Sustainability Control Tower, which enables administrators to restrict user access to specific sustainability metrics and measures. By leveraging SAP Cloud Identity Services, you can restrict visibility to particular business objects – such as metric actuals or measure actuals – and further narrow access by business location, legal entity, or organizational unit. This means each user only sees data relevant to them in apps like Manage ESG Data, Our Ambitions, the Emissions Dashboard, and the Analyze ESG Data app. Importantly, instance-based authorization augments rather than replaces existing SAP BTP roles and role collections, offering an additional layer of control for safeguarding sensitive ESG information. Getting started requires a subscription to SAP Cloud Identity Services, establishing trust, and then configuring or customizing authorization policies to align with your security and compliance needs. To learn more about these steps and see example policies, check out our updated documentation and discover how to determine who can view what within your sustainability landscape. 

 

Introducing the New Analyze ESG Data App 

As of February 2025, SCT has launched the Analyze ESG Data app, designed to replace the existing KPI tiles on the landing page and enhance how customers and partners interact with their ESG metrics. The new app provides robust filtering options, allowing users to select up to six time periods – by year, quarter, or month – and view their data in greater detail. Additionally, users can filter KPI tiles based on organizational hierarchies such as business location, reporting line, and legal entity. The app also offers flexible clustering and grouping of KPI tiles by subject matter (e.g., water, energy, waste), reporting frameworks (like ESRS or EU Taxonomy), through custom user-defined groups. Personalization features enable customers to save custom views with their chosen KPI tiles and apply authorization settings to ensure secure access. These updates aim to provide a more organized and tailored experience for tracking and managing ESG performance through the Analyze ESG Data app, now accessible in the SCT launchpad under the “Analytics and Steering” section. 

 

Customizable AI Templates 

In the Generate ESG Reports with AI app, you can now copy existing SAP-provided report templates and toggle individual sections on or off, as well as change the sectioning of the template structure. You can also see the status of an SAP-delivered template and get updates when available.  

 

Planned Features 

  • ESG reports with AI: You will be able to edit the generated content in-app before downloading the reports.  
  • Fiscal Period Support: You can soon integrate fiscal year and period configurations in S/4HANA Cloud into SAP Sustainability Control Tower. Integrations will allow you to upload data for the activated fiscal year variant and the defined fiscal periods. 
  • New supported languages: Italian and Turkish will be added to the supported languages at the end of Q1-2025. 

 

Additional Information 

 

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Control Tower, we recommend browsing the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

entry.title: Explore Netherlands EPR Declaration for 2024 with SAP Responsible Design and Production
entry.content:
We are thrilled to introduce a new report category in SAP Responsible Design and Production to support the mandate for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) reporting for Netherlands in 2024. This update ensures compliance with the latest regulatory requirements announced by Verpact.

 

SAP’s Circular Economy Vision

SAP is committed to transforming businesses and value chains for a circular economy, aiming for a future with zero waste. Our technology focuses on:

  • Eliminating waste and pollution
  • Circulating materials and keeping products in use
  • Regenerating natural systems

For more information, see Circular Economy Solutions. 

 

SAP Responsible Design and Production

SAP Responsible Design and Production provides intelligence that allows your company to monitor, measure, and act, so you can eliminate waste and circulate materials. SAP Responsible Design and Production calculates EPR obligations, plastic taxes, and corporate commitments to optimize material choices, reduce fees, and reduce risks coming from unforeseen regulatory costs and non-compliance. It enables you to make effective decisions with strategic data insights and analytics. 

For more information on the product or to request a demo, access SAP Responsible Design and Production.

 

Key Updates in the 2024 Netherlands EPR Regulation

The Netherlands has introduced significant changes to its EPR reporting requirements for 2024, affecting packaging producers and importers. Key changes include the following:

  • Updated Reporting Structure: The structure and format of EPR reports have been modified to align with the new regulatory framework, enabling you to keep up with the changing EU legislations.
  • New Packaging Categories: Additional types of packaging have been introduced as relevant for reporting, such as reusable cups and plastic bottles without deposit.
  • Enhanced Single-Use Plastic (SUP) Determination Logic: A revised methodology to identify and categorize single-use plastics for more accurate reporting.
  • Fee Modulation: New logic for discounts based on packaging material and recyclability.
  • Changes to EPR Fees for Packaging: Modifications affecting cost calculations for producers.

 

How SAP Responsible Design and Production Supports Compliance

Our new report category offers the following:

  • Automated Compliance Reporting: Generates reports adhering to the new mandate.
  • Customizable Data Inputs: Adapts reporting parameters based on business-specific packaging data.
  • Accurate Fee Calculation: Applies the latest EPR fee structures and modulations.
  • Enhanced Data Validation: Minimizes errors with built-in validation mechanisms.

 

For more details on this update and implementation, see Change in tariffs for packaging data reporting in 2024 in the Regulatory Change Manager, refer to our official documentation, or contact your account executive.

 

 

There are many features and functions which have been released in SAP Responsible Design and Production. You can check the What’s New section in the SAP Help Portal to know the latest updates for every new release.

For an outlook on upcoming regulatory changes and features, you can consult the Regulatory Change Manager and the SAP Road Map Explorer for SAP Responsible Design and Production. 

Stay up to date with EPR mandates and plastic taxes with SAP Responsible Design and Production!
